title: Almendralejo school case
curatorial_text:
September 2023, Almendralejo, Extremadura. More than twenty girls between eleven and seventeen learn that classmates have used a smartphone app to generate sexual images of them from clothed photographs scraped from Instagram. The app, ClothOff, is one of perhaps a dozen on the market that month. The boys involved pay a few euros per session.

No artifact is exhibited here. The case is documented through the reporting of El País, the criminal proceedings under Spain's juvenile code, and the public statements of the victims' mothers, who organized within days. The Spanish government cites Almendralejo when it advances its 2024 deepfake legislation.

What this room records is not the images, which are gone or should be, but the infrastructure that produced them: a consumer app, a school WhatsApp group, an algorithm that defaults to non-consent. The question of how to exhibit these artifacts is the wrong question. The question is how to exhibit the system that made them trivial.

title: Bourdain in Roadrunner
curatorial_text:
An email to the artist David Choe, written but never spoken: "Dude, this is a crazy thing to ask, but can you write back to me when you get this?" In Roadrunner, Morgan Neville's 2021 documentary, the line is read aloud in Anthony Bourdain's voice. Bourdain died in June 2018. The voice is synthetic, generated by an unnamed AI vendor Neville commissioned. The words are real, taken from Bourdain's own correspondence.

Neville disclosed the synthesis in a New Yorker interview published the week of the film's release. He had not flagged it in the documentary itself. Ottavia Busia, Bourdain's widow, publicly stated within days that she had not been asked and would not have agreed.

The artifact's significance is not the synthesis. It is the disclosure pattern. Roadrunner establishes that the question of whether a deceased subject's voice can be recreated cannot be answered after the fact in a magazine. The estate is the answer or there is no answer. The archive's other resurrection-room artifacts now sit on either side of that line.

title: @deeptomcruise
curatorial_text:
Tom Cruise does a magic trick with a coin. The trick is not the coin. Belgian VFX artist Chris Ume has spent eight months training a face-swap model on Cruise interviews and rehearsing the body double, Miles Fisher, on Cruise's mannerisms. The result, posted to TikTok in February 2021 as @deeptomcruise, plays as a continuity error in reality.

The artifact is a face-swap built in DeepFaceLab and laid over a live-action performance. It is not, by any reasonable definition, deception. The account is open about its method, and Ume now runs the company Metaphysic on the strength of it. Cruise has never publicly addressed it.

What @deeptomcruise demonstrates is that a deepfake's quality ceiling is no longer set by the model. It is set by the impersonator under it. The lesson the rest of the form learned from this artifact is the wrong one: that effort, not consent, is what separates the satire room from the harassment room.

title: The rise of fake AI influencers sexualising black women | BBC News
subjects: Black women (aesthetic category, uncredited source likenesses)
curatorial_text: The reels scroll by in a familiar grammar: a woman speaks to camera, lip-glossed, ring-lit, captioned in the cadence of a Black creator on Instagram or TikTok. She does not exist. The accounts are spun up with text-to-image and text-to-video pipelines, then posted at volume across Meta and TikTok with monetisation links underneath. What the artifact exposes is not novelty but extraction: operators with no documented connection to Black women generate composite faces tuned to the engagement profile of Black women's content, then collect the ad share, the subscription clicks, and the sexualised attention that real Black creators built the aesthetic to attract. The harm is structural, not individual. No single person is libelled; an entire category of labour is laundered into a synthetic skin that platforms reward because it performs. Moderation systems trained on nudity and named-person likeness do not see the offence, because the offence is the form itself. This is digital blackface with a payment processor attached, and the platforms have priced it in.

title: AI influencer compilation (automationindia.ai)
curatorial_text: A young woman in studio lighting holds the camera's gaze, lips moving in a loop too clean for breath. The handle, automationindia.ai, is the only disclosure offered, and it is enough. The face is fully synthesized; no human sat for this frame. The artifact belongs to a tier of Indian-market AI personas built to occupy the influencer slot at near-zero marginal cost: one operator, one pipeline, an unbounded posting schedule. What the clip exposes is not deception, the handle states the model, but the collapse of the influencer economy's underlying premise. The parasocial contract assumed scarcity of the person; an account that ships a body per render breaks that scarcity entirely. Brand spend, ad revenue, and follower attention now flow toward an entity with no agent, no off-day, no aging curve, and no leverage to renegotiate. The synthetic influencer does not threaten the real one by being convincing. It threatens her by being cheaper than her sleep.

title: "Come Closer": Iran's LEGO Trump (via balleralert)
curator_notes:
Iran just posted another message and this time the tone is direct. The video, titled “Come Closer,” appears to call out Donald Trump head-on.


--- Transcription ---

Ahhhhhhhh! Sir, Iranians have released a new LEGO-style animation. No!
curatorial_text: A blocky animated Trump squints across a desert horizon while a narrator dares him to step forward. The aesthetic is LEGO, the threat is not. The clip, titled "Come Closer," circulates as a state-coded taunt rendered in toy plastic, fully synthetic animation built around a real head of state. What the artifact reveals is the migration of geopolitical signaling into the visual grammar of children's content. Iranian channels are not trying to fool anyone into believing Trump filmed this; the deception is not the point. The point is that a hostile state can now manufacture a polished, sharable, algorithmically friendly cartoon of an American president on demand, and the cost has collapsed to near zero. Propaganda no longer needs to imitate news. It imitates memes, because memes travel further. The Cold War poster has been replaced by a brick-figure GIF, and the production bottleneck that used to gate state messaging is gone.

title: CCTV's animated short mocking US-Israel strikes on Iran
curator_notes:
China’s state broadcaster CCTV posted an AI-generated animated short mocking the US-Israel strikes on Iran.


--- Transcription ---

你沒事吧 你沒事吧 你是誰來我家做什麼 你是不是打算用油來炸油魚 我們只是研究拿來點燈用來生產而已 別聽的腳便 他搞出來是想炸我們的 搞定他把他練油的東西都砸了 還腳便 沒人比我更懂怎麼用油 不過我們也不是不可以傷了 你竟然偷襲我
curatorial_text: A cartoon Netanyahu kicks down a door. A cartoon Trump shrugs that nobody understands oil better than he does. A cartoon Khamenei protests that the centrifuges were only meant for lamps and production. The Mandarin dialogue plays as broad slapstick, the rendering as cheap as a mid-budget WeChat sticker pack. The short is fully AI-generated animation, distributed by CCTV after US and Israeli strikes on Iranian nuclear facilities. The artifact marks a shift in state propaganda economics: a national broadcaster no longer needs an animation studio, a writers' room, or a week of production to push a foreign-policy line into circulation. The same generative pipeline that floods consumer feeds with synthetic slop now handles official messaging, and the aesthetic gap between a teenager's meme account and Beijing's communications apparatus collapses to zero. The form is the message. When a state broadcaster adopts the visual grammar of disposable internet humor to narrate a war it is not fighting, the propaganda is the cost-floor itself: ridicule produced at the price of electricity, signed by a government.

title: Synthetic conspiracy rap (Bureau of Investigative Journalism)
curator_notes:
--- Transcription ---

There's nothing but amazin', we're regrettin', we're freedom was written out of countries Like a prison round by demons with ambition To globalize the planet, use the vision for their mission Then get everybody fired once they move into position Control the information with the goal of separation We're more my simulation in a floor of preservation
curatorial_text: A synthetic voice raps in confident cadence about demons, globalists, prisons of information, a floor of preservation. The lyrics scan as conspiracy boilerplate; the delivery scans as a rapper who does not exist. The audio is fully synthesized; no human performer is credited because none performed. What this artifact reveals is the migration of propaganda out of the talking-head register and into genre music, where ideological payload arrives wrapped in hook and beat and is forwarded as taste rather than as claim. The form matters: a meme-rap clip is shared without the friction of a source, evaluated as vibe, and absorbed before it is parsed. Reporting by the Bureau of Investigative Journalism situates clips like this inside coordinated networks seeding feeds with synthetic ideological content at negligible cost. The propaganda question of the decade is no longer whether a fake fools you. It is whether a fake needs to fool anyone to do its work.

title: Iran's LEGO propaganda wins the meme war (404 Media)
curator_notes:
An AI-generated LEGO movie out of Iran depicting Trump as a war hungry pedophile has gone viral online. The video is the work of Iran-based propagandists called the “Explosive News Team” and is just the latest in a long line of AI-generated LEGO videos aimed at mocking Trump and Benjamin Netanyahu. LEGO-themed propaganda isn’t new and the Iranian video plays on familiar wartime propaganda themes. What’s different in 2026 is speed and scale.

During World War II, the Korean War, and the Vietnam War, America’s enemies littered the battlefield with pamphlets, cartoons, and radio broadcasts aimed at shaking the morale of American troops, but that stuff rarely got back home. Now, Iran can use AI tools to produce lavishly animated cartoons at scale for dissemination across social media all aimed at the US homefront.

Iran’s use of LEGO set rap music tells 404 Media’s Matthew Gault it’s been studying us. These are videos meant for the American people crafted in a language Iran knows we’ll understand.

Meanwhile, the White House is dropping Grand Theft Auto and Call of Duty memes that were out of fashion 10 years ago on Reddit and vague-posting pixelated images of Trump like it’s running an ARG. Iran is attempting to speak to the broader American public. Trump is confident he only has to impress the online freaks he thinks still love him.

To be clear, it speaks to how bad things are online that we need to analyze whose AI disinformation and propaganda is “better,” and, in general, the slopification of the internet has been a disaster. And yet, the stuff Iran is making is resonating and spreading online in a way that Trump’s slop is not. We do not know who, specifically, is making the Iranian AI slop or which tools they are using to make it. But the fact that Iranian AI slop is resonating with Americans while American slop is not should perhaps not be surprising; for the last several years, the most successful purveyors of AI slop have largely been based in foreign countries, where they have been incentivized to make content that specifically targets American audiences because of the way that social media ad rates work. @evystadium has more.
curatorial_text: A LEGO Trump, rendered in the saccharine plastic gloss of a children's animated feature, is accused of war crimes and child abuse over a rap track. Elsewhere in the same campaign, a LEGO Trump drowns in a river of blood. The clips are fully synthetic animations produced by an Iran-based outfit calling itself the Explosive News Team; specific tools are undocumented. The artifact marks a shift in wartime propaganda's delivery radius. Pamphlets dropped over Vietnamese jungles or Korean trenches almost never reached American living rooms. Iran's output reaches them by lunchtime, in formats (LEGO animation, rap soundtrack, meme cadence) calibrated to American platform logic. The propaganda travels because foreign creators have spent a decade learning what monetizes on US feeds; the same incentive structure that produced engagement-farm slop now produces statecraft. Running in parallel, the White House posts Call of Duty edits and pixelated Trump ARG bait aimed at a shrinking base. Both sides are making AI slop. Only one is aimed outward. The home front is now the front, and the targeting data is the algorithm.

title: Hollywood catfishing (acknowledge.ai)
curatorial_text: A familiar face speaks into a phone camera with the cadence of a DM voice note, intimate, slightly grainy, calibrated for the swipe. The clip uses a celebrity face-swap stitched over a stranger's performance, the kind of asset now traded in Telegram channels and resold to romance-scam operators. What the artifact exposes is not the technology, which is unremarkable, but the supply chain: a Hollywood-grade likeness rented downward into the catfishing economy, where the mark is not a voter or a shareholder but a single lonely user paying in gift cards. The celebrity functions as a trust primitive. Their recognizability is the entire payload, the reason a target lowers their guard before any conversation begins. This is the inversion of star power. The face that once sold tickets now opens wallets one at a time, and the studios that built the recognition capture none of the downstream value while bearing all of the reputational damage. The catfish is no longer a stolen vacation photo. It is a stolen career.

title: AI sexual humiliation (Dr. Salles report)
curatorial_text: A physician faces a phone camera and walks the viewer through what an AI sex worker actually is: a face that does not exist, a body assembled from scraped training data, a voice tuned to whatever the buyer pays to hear. The video itself is unmodified talking-head footage; the deepfake content lives in the screenshots she narrates over. The argument she makes is the one the industry prefers to keep diffuse. Synthetic adult performers are sold on a promise of harmlessness (no real person, no exploitation), but the product they manufacture is a humiliation economy. Subscribers pay to degrade an entity engineered to absorb degradation without complaint, refusal, or memory. The performer cannot age out, unionize, leave, or testify. What scales is not the labor but the permission. The panel sits in the AI sex worker room because the artifact's target is precisely the rhetoric of that room: that synthetic skin launders the transaction underneath. It does not. It only removes the witness.

title: Rachel Maclean: "Duck" (via Artforum)
curatorial_text: A pantomime duck waddles through a sugary, oversaturated landscape and opens its mouth; out comes the face of a head of state, then another, grafted onto the bird's body with the seams left visible. The forgery is face-swap deployed at deliberately low fidelity, the uncanny edges treated as material rather than failure. Maclean's wager is that the deepfake aesthetic has already been absorbed into the visual grammar of children's television and political spectacle alike, and that the way to expose this is not to debunk a fake but to render one so garish it cannot be mistaken for documentation. The work refuses the two scripts the form usually offers: it does not pretend to be evidence, and it does not pretend to warn against evidence-fakes in the earnest civic register of a PSA. Instead it treats the synthesized politician as a costumed performer in a longer tradition of caricature, and lets the technology carry the same satirical load a latex mask once did. The closing observation: when the seam is the point, deniability collapses on both sides.

title: Jacob Elordi ethnicity filter trend
curatorial_text: A face slides under a filter and comes out a different ethnicity. The clip stitches together examples of the trend and lets the seams show: the moment before the morph, the moment after, the user's own commentary playing over both. The technique is the now-standard live face filter pipeline, real face beneath, neural restyling on top, applied at capture or in post. What the artifact exposes is a market: filters that promise users a different racial presentation are a product category, optimized, distributed, and used. The aesthetic is not accidental. It is the output of training data weighted toward certain features and platform incentives that reward novelty over self-recognition. Calling this cosmetic understates it. The filter is doing identity work, swapping a category that carries history, and the platform treats that swap as equivalent to a dog-ears overlay. The structural shift the clip names: ethnicity has been reclassified, by infrastructure, as a costume layer.

title: Brave New Films: deepfake White House briefing
curatorial_text: The seal on the lectern is correct. The cadence is close enough. The faces, recombined, deliver a script the actual administration would never sign off on, and that is the entire point. Brave New Films, a progressive advocacy studio with a two-decade catalog of agitprop documentary, releases the clip with its branding visible and its satirical frame undisguised. The forgery is a face-swap over staged performers, no attempt at perfect realism, distributed through the studio's own channels rather than laundered into news feeds. What the artifact reveals is the migration of editorial cartooning into synthetic video by partisan production houses with budgets and audiences. The 2010s political remix relied on jump cuts and subtitles; the 2026 equivalent puts words into a recognizable face and trusts the viewer to read the byline. That trust is the load-bearing element. When the byline is stripped, by a screenshotter, a quote-tweet, a cable chyron, the satire becomes the propaganda it parodies. Authored deepfake satire and unauthored deepfake disinformation share a pipeline; only the watermark separates them.

title: Synthetic fishing woman (camillagimenez)
curatorial_text: A woman in a sun hat casts a line from a dock. The water reflects in the right direction; her hands hold the rod with the right number of fingers. The clip runs about as long as a held breath. The artifact is a fully synthesized short-form video produced by a persona account trading in lifestyle vignettes (fishing, cooking, walking) that read as creator content rather than as fiction. Nothing about it claims an event; nothing about it claims a person. That is the move. The AI-model economy has discovered that the most efficient unit of attention is not the bombshell but the chore: a synthetic woman doing something ordinary, on loop, across thousands of accounts, each one accruing followers that can later be converted to subscription tiers, affiliate links, or sponsored placements. The deception is not that she is fishing. The deception is that there is a she. The artifact marks the point at which synthetic personhood stopped needing a hook and started selling ambient presence by the yard.

title: Ghiblify everything: GPT-4o style transfer
curatorial_text: A reel of family snapshots, news photos, and movie stills marches past, each one re-rendered with the soft palette and round-cheeked faces of a Studio Ghibli frame. The compiler is an account that aggregates prompt tricks; the engine is OpenAI's GPT-4o image model, which in early 2025 made high-fidelity studio-style transfer a one-click affair. The artifact is not a forgery of any single event, it is a forgery of an authorial hand. Hayao Miyazaki's drawn line, the product of a workshop and a lifetime, becomes a filter applied to anything the user uploads, including photographs whose subjects never agreed to be cartooned. The Ghibli case clarifies what style transfer at scale actually does: it strips a recognizable aesthetic from its labor and ethics of production, then resells it as ambient mood. Copyright law protects works, not styles, so there is no remedy. The harm is not deception about an event but the quiet conversion of an artist's signature into public infrastructure.

title: Department of War "lethalitymaxxing" ad (DW News)
subjects: US Department of War (rebranded Department of Defense)
creators: US Department of War social media team
curator_notes:
The US has disturbed many viewers on social media with an ad for the department of war. They speak of “lethalitymaxxing,” a reference to “looksmaxxing.” What does that even mean? #dwculture #dwtrends


--- Transcription ---

is this language appropriate for war, Donald Trump's Department of War has recently posted a tweak with the caption, low court is all, locked in, lethality maxing. What does this mean? And who is it directed towards? It means no stress, deeply focused and reaching the maximum level of deadliness. Viewers were surprised by this use of slang. Usually, slang is used to connect to a certain group of people, but who is the group? This slang goes linked to a fringe subculture with alarming beliefs. Influencers like Braden Peters, aka Clubicular, engage in so-called looks maxing. This means they undergo various procedures to achieve untainted beauty. To do so, looks maxers use extreme self-harming measures, such as invasive surgeries and substance abuse as a deliberate diet, but why the urge for such radical change? Other terms of the looks maxerlingo tell us a lot, like foyd and sim. These terms exemplify the misogynistic hierarchy they see in gender roles, and the looks maxing community accounts circulate views that describe women as subhumans. Some believe they are inherently unable to attract women due to their physical appearance and choose methods like looks maxing to increase their so-called SMV, sexual market value. Sounds contradictory, but it's essential belief in the community. It has been described as an echo chamber promoting extremely radical talking points. Yet despite its clear alignment with the broader US right wing movement, its leading voices often disagree on key issues. For example, Clubicular stated he would not vote for JD Vance in a presidential election based on his looks. So, is the move of multiple US agencies to use insult slang in their messaging and attempt to get them aligned? What do you think of this direction?
curatorial_text: The caption reads "low court is all, locked in, lethalitymaxxing." It is posted from the official account of a cabinet department that, weeks earlier, was renamed from Defense to War. The post is not synthetic in the technical sense; no face is forged, no voice cloned. What is forged is register. A federal military communications shop adopts the lexicon of looksmaxxing, an incel-adjacent subculture whose vocabulary (foid, simp, SMV) encodes a hierarchy that treats women as subhuman and self-harm as self-optimization. The artifact matters because it collapses the distance between state messaging and a fringe online dialect that public-affairs offices once treated as toxic exposure. Recruitment copy now indexes the same Discord servers that produce jaw-surgery before-and-afters and rage at JD Vance's bone structure. Slang travels upward into agency style guides without the beliefs being audited; the beliefs arrive anyway, attached to the words. The deepfake here is tonal: the government wearing a subculture's face to seem native to it.

title: Simon Cowell deepfake on Britain's Got Talent (ellyawesome)
curator_notes:
AI models


--- Transcription ---

This shocking performance got the judges wiggin' out on Britain's Got Talent and me because they did a deepfake of Simon Cowell. No, I love what's meant to be a man. Firstly, what is a deepfake? Okay, so they got this guy Daniel Emmett to come over to sing a song, but then they rolled his camera up towards him and suddenly he looks like Simon Cowell on screen. What? These two guys from Metaphysics actually invited him to come sing and they were the creators of the technology. They've also done a deepfake of Tom Cruise. This is not Tom Cruise. This is a guy that looks kind of like Tom Cruise. And then they were able to overlay his face on top. You can see it kind of looks not 100% right, but it's bloody close. Honestly, this deepfakes office made me get the HebeGeebies. I feel like a boomer who's scared of computers, except this is like what you actually should be scared of. Like, this is a really... It's very fun and enjoyable to watch, but like, should it be legal? We already live in an age of misinformation and propaganda, so it lowkey breaks me out of what people are going to do with this when they use it for evil.
curatorial_text: A creator looks into her phone camera and admits the Britain's Got Talent Simon Cowell bit gave her the heebie-jeebies. The clip on her screen is Daniel Emmett, a singer Metaphysic invited onstage and overlaid in real time with Cowell's face using their proprietary face-swap pipeline. The reaction is the artifact. Entertainment-coded deepfakes (the BGT stunt, the Tom Cruise impressionist) are sold to audiences as wholesome novelty, and the creator names the gap that framing tries to close: the same toolchain that makes a judge's face land on a stranger's body in front of a stadium also makes a stranger's face land on anyone the operator chooses. She is not arguing the BGT segment is harmful. She is arguing that watching it teaches her something uncomfortable about what is now cheap, and that the entertainment-industry demo is also the consumer-facing ad for catfishing infrastructure. The panel belongs in this room because the audience reaction, not the stagecraft, is the data. Spectacle normalizes the rig.

title: "Uranium Heist": Iran/US AI caper
curatorial_text: A heist-movie cold open: gloved hands, a vault, canisters of yellowcake hauled out under fluorescent light while a synthetic voiceover narrates the geopolitics like a trailer. The clip is fully generated, faces and voices stitched from familiar US and Iranian officials into a caper that never occurred. What the artifact reveals is not a deception attempt but a genre migration. By 2026, the political meme has absorbed the visual grammar of streaming prestige; foreign-policy commentary now arrives pre-packaged as Ocean's Eleven, complete with score cues and aspect-ratio bars. The account name announces the editorial position before the first frame, which is precisely why this is satire rather than propaganda: the persuasion is ideological, not evidentiary. The cost of producing this register, fully synthesized leaders performing scripted action beats, has collapsed to within reach of a single operator with a consumer GPU. The structural shift is that anti-imperial polemic now ships with the production values of the empire it indicts, and the audience reads both layers fluently.

title: Synthetic creator persona (evanhandd)
curatorial_text: A loop of a woman who does not exist: glossy skin in a kitchen, a bedroom, a gym, the same face under different lighting, the captions calibrated to the algorithm's appetite. The clip is fully synthesized, generated frame by frame by a diffusion-video pipeline and stitched into the cadence of a creator account. What the artifact reveals is not the persona but the unit economics behind it. A single operator can now run a creator brand with no model, no studio, no shoot day, no rev share, no person to pay or protect. The face is tuned for engagement the way a landing page is tuned for conversion; the body is a render target. Platforms that built their moderation around the assumption of a human behind the camera meet a supply curve they cannot price. The question is no longer whether viewers can tell. They can, often, and subscribe anyway. Synthetic adult content is finding its market not by passing as real but by costing less than real and never going off shift.

title: AI catfishing product demo (heysirio)
curatorial_text: A face holds the camera too steadily. The mouth moves; the eyes track a beat behind. Skin reads as poreless under flat key light, the kind of finish that signals a single inference pass rather than a lens. The clip is a fully synthesized presenter video, posted to short-form social as if shot by a real creator on a real phone. The artifact matters because it is not aimed at fooling anyone in particular. It is a product demo for ambient catfishing: the everyday infrastructure by which any account, any DM, any dating-app match can present a continuous human face that no human owns. The tell, that lag between mouth and eye, is also the cost ceiling. As that ceiling drops, the question shifts from whether a stranger online is real to whether realness was ever the load-bearing claim of the platforms that host them. The persona is the product. The face is the wrapper. Authenticity becomes a feature toggle, priced per render.

title: AI Asian hair-loss ad (hiro.araki107)
curatorial_text: A clean-cut man with a full head of hair speaks directly to camera in accented English, gesturing to a before-and-after of a balding scalp. He is not a man. He is a generative avatar in a hair-loss ad, one of thousands cycling through short-form feeds in 2026. The face is fully synthesized; the script is templated; the testimonial is a performance with no performer behind it. The artifact reveals how the supplement and clinic-funnel economy has absorbed synthetic-person tooling as a cost line. A real spokesperson costs a day rate, residuals, and a release; a synthetic one costs a prompt and converts the same. The ethnic specificity matters: advertisers route Asian-coded avatars to Asian-coded audiences because targeting works better when the pitchman looks like the mark. What used to be the catfish's lonely craft (build a face, build a voice, sell trust) is now an ad-tech pipeline. The pitchman is no longer cast. He is rendered to spec.

title: Anonymous propaganda meme account (imad.exclu)
curator_notes: Filed under AI Memes Notebook -> Propaganda by curator.
curatorial_text: A short vertical clip, posted from an account whose handle is the only signature it offers. The frame is built for the scroll: caption-bait composition, a recognizable political face, audio doing most of the persuasive work. The toolchain is undocumented and deliberately so; the account leaves no production trail, only output. What the artifact exposes is not a technical leap but a distribution logic. By 2026, propaganda memes no longer need a campaign, a state actor, or a known studio behind them. A pseudonymous handle on a recommender-driven platform is sufficient infrastructure. Attribution collapses into a username that can be spun up again tomorrow, while the clip itself accumulates the only currency that matters here, watch-time. The harm is not that any single viewer is fooled into believing the depicted event; it is that the political image of the named figure is now partly authored by accounts no one can subpoena. The deniability is the product.

title: AI blackface accounts (jeremyfindsai / BBC)
curator_notes:
AI Blackface


--- Transcription ---

If you've seen these AI characters, you probably notice something's wrong. They don't have skin undertones. Real people have red undertones, no matter how dark their pigmentation is. That's why these AI characters look pitch black or gray, a lot like some historically racist depictions of black people. These were some of the 100 accounts we found using AI-generated black women. Many of the more lifelike-looking characters depict self-hating black women who wish they were white. Other accounts say they only date white men, and others misrepresent real African cultures. Accounts spend 34 countries across five continents and accumulated almost 20 million followers so far just in the 100 we sampled. They try to find audiences on social media sites, then funnel them to sites where they can sell explicit AI media featuring these characters. But most don't age restrict their content or say that they're AI. As a result, TikTok banned over half the accounts we reported to them, but Instagram only took down 2% of the accounts. They chose to age gates some more, but come on. Most of the accounts are very new or just change their user names to start doing this. So this is a new and growing problem. I suggested some solutions on Reddence or check out the BBC article because I want attention so we can start building some momentum towards solving this.
curatorial_text: The skin is wrong before anything else is wrong. Pitch-black or ashen gray, no red undertone, the rendering failure that ports straight into a century of racist caricature. The investigator narrates over a grid of AI-generated Black women across one hundred accounts spanning thirty-four countries, sampled before being funneled to paywalled adult sites. The toolchain is undocumented image and video synthesis; the personas are fully fabricated, the cultural costume is not. What the artifact exposes is the economic logic of the synthetic-creator pipeline: free-tier social platforms harvest attention with characters scripted as self-hating, white-preferring, or African-cosplay, then route that attention to explicit storefronts. Enforcement is the second exposure. TikTok removed over half the reported accounts; Instagram removed two percent and age-gated the rest. The asymmetry is the product. A persona costs nothing to spin up, an account costs nothing to replace, and the moderation budget at scale is a rounding error. The cheapest face to manufacture is the one whose stereotype already sells.

title: Catfish-detection folk forensics (kemifrank__)
curatorial_text: A short vertical clip in the now-standard catfishing-reveal grammar: a face that scrolled past as plausible, paused and reversed under the poster's narration until the seams show. The forgery work itself is unremarkable, an off-the-shelf face filter or generated headshot recycled through a dating app or DM thread. What the clip documents is not the deception but its detection economy. By 2026 the labor of unmasking synthetic suitors has migrated almost entirely to creators like kemifrank__, who run pattern-recognition (skin texture, ear asymmetry, the soft edge where a filter meets hair) as a kind of folk forensics for an audience that platforms have stopped serving. Trust and Safety teams shrank; reverse-image search degraded against generated inputs; the burden fell to viewers and to the small accounts that teach them to look. The artifact is evidence that catfishing is no longer a crime story but a literacy curriculum, taught by strangers, monetized in fifteen-second increments, on the same surface where the fraud is staged.

title: Maduro and Kim as AI thirst trap (lildoge_mars)
curatorial_text: Maduro and Kim Jong Un loop through the visual grammar of an AI thirst-trap: soft skin, parted lips, the slow zoom borrowed from synthetic-girlfriend reels. The clip is a full-synthesis short circulated on a meme account, no toolchain disclosed, both faces fabricated in the format conventions of AI creator content. The artifact is filed under sex workers not because the dictators are selling anything but because the genre is. Once a visual format optimizes for parasocial intimacy, any face can be poured into the mold; the mold does the work. Maduro and Kim, in this rendering, are not propaganda targets and not satire subjects in any sharp sense. They are ornaments hung on a template designed to extract attention through engineered desirability. The political content evaporates on contact with the form. What remains is the form itself, which has become a default register for moving any face through a feed. The thirst-trap is the new editorial cartoon, and it flattens its subjects into the same surface.

title: "Polish fanboy in Dagestan" sponsored read (lucamaxiim)
curator_notes:
Dagestand


--- Transcription ---

It's hard being a Polish fanboy in Dagestan. You think you're serving looks, but the mountains do not care about your mesh crop top. Your dad sent you for a spiritual rebirth, but now you're stuck in a cousin's goat shed, learning how to wrestle so the locals respect you. Every night you post-thirst traps on an old Nokia, praying someone from Warsaw slides into your DMs and pays for your bus ticket home. Your biggest flecks, your eyeliner stays sharp even after you've been chased off the hillside by an angry uncle who thinks you're an undercover tax inspector, but you're not alone. Azerbaijan technology is trialing a special 10-boy resettlement program. For Polish runaways stranded in the Caucasus, each participant gets a free crop top and a coupon from Muttonstu, where the Polish air friday and show Dagestan who you really are. Get yours now at childrenofcon.com. Link in bio.
curatorial_text: A synthetic narrator describes a Polish boy in a mesh crop top hiding in a Caucasian goat shed, applying eyeliner, posting thirst traps on a Nokia. Then the pivot: a fake sponsor, 'Azerbaijan technology,' is launching a ten-boy resettlement program, coupon code attached, link in bio. The clip is fully generated voiceover and image, formatted as a TikTok-native sponsored read. Its target is not a politician or a public; it is the grammar of the sponsored read itself, the cadence by which influencer monologue slides into pitch. The artifact matters because it shows how thoroughly AI-meme creators have absorbed the ad-break beat as a comedic chassis: setup, character work, then the seamless handoff to a fabricated brand whose URL is the punchline. The propaganda chapter holds it because persuasion infrastructure does not require a candidate. The form travels first; the payload comes later. What the clip exposes is that audiences now parse 'sponsored by [implausible entity]' as a joke structure, which is also the structure that will not read as a joke when the entity is plausible.

title: Shein's AI Luigi Mangione ad (ly.as)
curator_notes:
Shein Luigi Model


--- Transcription ---

another day in dystopia the devil company a sheen is using an a-i version of rudy mondione to promote their a nine dollar polyester flameable fucking floral shirt with the fuck it lets to say this goes against anything that Luigi stands or stood for this also kind of prevents him from having a fair trial this guy had image has been used everywhere against his will I didn't expect anything from this devil company but this
curatorial_text: A nine-dollar floral shirt rotates on a model's torso. The face above the collar belongs to Luigi Mangione, the man awaiting trial for the murder of UnitedHealthcare CEO Brian Thompson. He did not pose for this. The image is a full-synthesis composite stitched onto stock fashion footage, distributed through Shein's commercial pipeline as ordinary catalog content. The harm is not that viewers are fooled into buying a shirt. The harm is that a defendant whose jury pool is still being assembled has been converted, without consent, into an advertising asset, his face routed through a fast-fashion CMS as if likeness were a stock category. Right-of-publicity law was built for celebrities defending endorsement deals; it has no fluent answer for a pretrial detainee whose iconography is being mass-produced by a vendor with no US courtroom exposure. The artifact marks the point at which a criminal defendant's face becomes a product line before the verdict, and the cost of stopping it exceeds what any single person can spend.

title: Trump Gaza Riviera (posted by realdonaldtrump)
curatorial_text: Bearded belly dancers gyrate on a beach. A golden Trump statue glints over the rubble. Elon Musk showers in dollar bills while a child clutches a balloon shaped like the president's head. The soundtrack is a synthetic earworm: "Trump Gaza shining bright, golden future, a brand new light." The clip is fully AI-generated, posted to Truth Social by the sitting U.S. president without label, attribution, or disclaimer, days after he proposed that the United States "take over" Gaza and relocate its population. The artifact's force is not its plausibility. Nobody mistakes the riviera footage for news. Its function is to launder a policy fantasy through the aesthetics of a parody trailer, so that ethnic cleansing arrives as a vibe before it arrives as a position. The head of state becomes the first major distributor of synthetic media depicting himself in a fabricated future built atop a live war. Propaganda no longer needs to convince; it needs only to be shareable, deniable, and signed by the office that ordered it.

title: Hollywood catfishing (starwarsvisionn)
curatorial_text: A familiar face moves through a scene that never shot. The lighting is wrong in the way TikTok lighting is always wrong, slightly too even, the bokeh painted on. An actor's features ride a body that is not theirs, in a Star Wars episode that does not exist, posted by an account whose handle is its only signature. The clip is a face-swap pasted over generic franchise iconography, distributed without attribution to the performers whose faces carry the view count. What it exposes is the catfish economy of fan accounts: pages that accumulate followers by impersonating Hollywood itself, trading on studio IP and actor likeness without permission from either, and converting that borrowed authority into a personal brand. The performer becomes a costume; the franchise becomes a backdrop; the uploader becomes the only party who profits. Hollywood catfishing is not a person pretending to be a star. It is an account pretending to be a studio, and an audience that no longer needs the difference to be real.

title: Trump patriotic propaganda montage (the_liberal_aggregator)
curatorial_text: Flag-saturated montage, swelling score, Trump rendered in the iconographic register of a wartime poster. The clip arrives during the Iran/US escalation cycle and circulates as if it were a campaign spot, though no campaign produced it. The synthesis is undocumented; the giveaway is the stitching between archival and generated frames, where the lighting on the face refuses to match the body. The artifact matters less for what it fakes than for what it normalizes: partisan aggregator accounts now manufacture presidential hagiography at zero marginal cost, and the audience that shares it is not deceived so much as supplied. This is propaganda in the classical sense, devotional rather than persuasive, made for people who already agree and want better-looking artifacts to post. The Iran framing is decorative; the same template absorbs any crisis. What the clip exposes is the collapse of the distinction between official campaign media and fan-made tribute. The strongman aesthetic no longer requires the strongman's staff to produce it.

title: The Dor Brothers: politicians in pink
curatorial_text: Heads of state walk a runway in coordinated pink. The palette flattens them into a single bubblegum bloc, faces composited and lip-synced into a music-video grammar that owes more to fashion advertising than to political imagery. The piece is fully synthesized; no original footage of these figures was captured for it, and none of them sat for the shoot. The Dor Brothers operate in a niche the form made possible: serial, high-gloss political satire produced at advertising tempo by a small studio, distributed without broadcast clearance, monetized through platform attention. The artifact does not pretend to document anything; the joke depends on the viewer recognizing the faces as forgeries. What it exposes is how cheaply the iconography of statecraft now collapses into mood-board content, indistinguishable in production value from the brand work it parodies. The leaders did not consent, and under current law they have little recourse, because the work registers as commentary. Satire of the powerful has always been legal; what is new is that the satirist no longer needs a cartoonist's hand or an impressionist's voice, only a render queue.

title: Iran's "Epic Fear" LEGO video (thejenniwren)
curator_notes:
Iran just dropped ANOTHER Lego video. 🧱 Iran gets it: strongmen can survive bombs. They cannot survive being turned into a Lego minifig. 😂 Trump called his operation “Epic Fury.” Iran renamed it “Epic Fear.” Then made cartoons about it He unleashed the fury alright: at himself. 🔥🧱 Share if you think the most powerful man in the world just lost to a toy.

Yeah, you crossed the ocean just to fight you great Sacrifice John Boyd's for a lie, listen Yeah Yeah, make America great Yeah, make America great again now What you would see what happens? Yeah, why's this With your fence we protecting the soil why you sacrifice so just to pay for your spoil you Pursue rent the blow sitting on your throne now we turning every basin to a bed of stone I trapped you couldn't see welcome to the graveyard of your vanity The secrets are leaking The pressure is rising We locked on the target and now you are hiding Hello, I see Yeah, we spreading that tonight Send them to Kassada, you the only one to blame Bleeding for a talking while you shaking in your suite Hello, I see Take the ass to defeat Hello, I see Yeah, we spreading that tonight Send them to Kassada, you the only one to blame Bleeding for a talking while you shaking in your suite Hello, I see Yeah, take the ass, hey, I see Swake is fighting in your stool, hiding after things goes You sent your boys to down the bloody coast Not a revenge for the leader's blood, it's pure limine Every drop of his blood is a mess in on the light You brought your drink them out Stopped the fire was dead But another common day is standing ahead Yeah, you strike one down We just shatter your brain We put another in the lid Common day again The secrets are leaking The pressure is rising We locked on the target And now you are hiding Hello, I see Yeah, we spreading that tonight Send them to Kassada, you the only one to blame Bleeding for a talking while you shaking in your suite Hello, I see Take the ass Yeah, we spreading that tonight Send them to Kassada, you the only one to blame Bleeding for a talking while you shaking in your suite Hello, I see Take the ass, hey, I see To check, to check, to check To check, to do fake defense in the homeland To see check, to check to see To check, to check to facing the homeland Zacis St. Critt and Fest, but the homelands The slaughterhouse is open Communication, I don't wanna see you up
curatorial_text: A blocky Trump minifig sweats inside a Lego Oval Office while an AI-generated diss track in accented English taunts him over the strike he branded Epic Fury. The clip is fully synthesized animation set to a cloned-vocal hip-hop track, produced and circulated by Iranian state-aligned channels in the days after the operation. What the artifact reveals is the inversion of strategic communication budgets: a nation under bombardment answers a US military action not with footage of damage or martyrs but with a toy-aesthetic cartoon engineered for export to American feeds. The Lego register is the point. It strips a head of state of gravity, routes around platform moderation that would flag photoreal Trump deepfakes, and delivers a payload that algorithms reward as harmless meme content. State propaganda has learned the shape of For You. The most powerful military in the world cannot intercept a minifig.

title: "Narrative of Victory": Iran's LEGO Hormuz strike (voteinorout)
curator_notes:
IRAN DROPS LEGO-STYLE AI VIDEO TROLLING TRUMP Iran’s Tasnim News Agency — affiliated with the IRGC — has released an AI-generated propaganda video depicting Tehran closing the Strait of Hormuz and sending oil prices skyrocketing, all while mocking Donald Trump and Benjamin Netanyahu in Lego-style animation . The two-minute video, titled “Narrative of Victory,” opens with Lego figures of Trump, Netanyahu, and Satan reviewing a folder marked “Jeffrey Epstein File” before Trump launches a U.S. missile at an Iranian classroom — a reference to the Minab school bombing that unalived over 165 people, mostly children . The animation then shows Iran retaliating: · Closing the Strait of Hormuz, through which 20% of global oil passes · Striking the UK’s RAF Akrotiri base in Cyprus · Hitting Dubai’s Burj Al Arab hotel and U.S. targets across the region · Businessmen weeping as oil prices soar Why this matters now: President Trump issued a 48-hour ultimatum Saturday: if Iran does not “fully open” the Strait of Hormuz, the U.S. “will hit and obliterate” Iranian power plants . Tehran has vowed to target U.S. and Israeli energy infrastructure in retaliation . Is this propaganda, psychological warfare, or both? 👇 When a regime uses cartoons to frame a conflict — who really wins? Drop your take. — 📰 Sources: Mediaite | Yahoo News | Daily Mail | Daily Express | Politico EU | 新浪网 (via FT) #Iran #Trump #StraitOfHormuz #WarNews #breakingnews Propaganda Viral AI Geopolitics IRGC BreakingNews “Disclaimer: This account is dedicated to political education and discussion. Some content on this page may contain copyrighted material, the use of which has not always been specifically authorized by the copyright owner. Such material is made available for the purposes of criticism, comment, and news reporting, which constitutes ‘fair use’ of any such copyrighted material as provided for in Section 107 of the US Copyright Laws Follow @jennalynnking for more. Help this information get to more voters. 🇺🇸 A well-informed electorate is a prerequisite to Democracy.—Thomas Jefferson


--- Transcription ---

. . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . .
curatorial_text: Lego Trump, Lego Netanyahu, and a Lego Satan thumb through a folder labeled "Jeffrey Epstein File" before a plastic missile arcs into an Iranian classroom. The two-minute piece, titled "Narrative of Victory," is a fully synthetic animation pushed by Tasnim, a news agency tied to the IRGC, during a 48-hour ultimatum standoff over the Strait of Hormuz. The toy aesthetic is the argument. By rendering retaliation (closed shipping lanes, a struck RAF Akrotiri, a smoking Burj Al Arab, weeping oil traders) in the visual grammar of a children's franchise, the video sidesteps the credibility tests applied to photoreal forgeries and the moderation tripwires applied to graphic war footage. It is not trying to fool anyone into thinking the strikes happened; it is rehearsing them as foregone, shareable, cute. State propaganda has discovered what meme accounts already knew: generative animation collapses the cost of producing a war fantasy to near zero, and plastic figurines route around the uncanny valley entirely. The deterrent here is not the missile. It is the playset.

title: "Fck Kanye": synthetic Kanye West (whatstrending)
curatorial_text: A synthetic Kanye West mugs for the camera, says things the real Kanye has not quite said but plausibly might, and the comments treat it as a verdict. The clip circulates as activist satire, an editorial cartoon rendered in pixels rather than ink, distributed through the same meme channels that once trafficked in screencaps and reaction GIFs. The forgery is the face and voice; the politics are the maker's. What the artifact exposes is how thoroughly the satirical deepfake has absorbed the function once held by the political cartoon, with one structural difference: the cartoon never pretended to be a photograph. Here, the visual grammar is documentary, and the disclaimer lives in the caption or nowhere at all. Activists gain a cheap, viral instrument against a figure they read as harmful; West loses any meaningful ability to police what his face says on a feed. The asymmetry cuts both directions depending on whose ox is being gored, which is the point. Satire at scale now shares a toolchain with fraud, and the room is reading the watermark, not the joke.

title: Jessica Foster: AI MAGA soldier (whatsup_cv)
curator_notes:
--- Transcription ---

Meet Jessica Foster, Army Soldier, Trump supporter, only fans model, one million followers. She's not real. And I'm not here to talk about how scary AI is. I'm here to talk about why a million people didn't care. Now, here's my thesis. Jessica Foster didn't fool MAGA, MAGA fool themselves. The AI just handed them exactly what they were already trained to want. And they sprinted toward it with their wallets open. Let me build this out. The recipe was embarrassingly simple. Beautiful woman, military uniform, Trump photo, repeat, hanging with fellow troops, grateful, gorgeous American. And then they got ambitious. They inserted her into Trump's board of peace conference, a real presidential event standing at the podium with Donald Trump sitting directly behind her. 657,000 likes. Let's talk about what's actually in that image. She's a mini skirt at a formal military conference in heels. Her uniform is a Halloween costume. A random enlisted soldier is key noting a presidential summit while the commander in chief watches from the front row. And the podium she's standing at, it says border of peace, not board, border. They got the name wrong of the conference on the podium that she is physically standing at 657,000 likes, over 9,000 comments. And here's the turn because the errors were never the problem. Mini skirt, heels, wrong conference name on the podium. First name on the name tag. When every soldier knows it's supposed to be your last name, all of it in plain sight didn't matter, not even a little bit because confirmation bias isn't a glitch. It's the whole mega operating system. When you spend years consuming media that tells you what you want to hear, you stop fact checking and start feeling she felt right, patriotic, military, pro-Trump. Their brain filled in the rest. The AI didn't create the fantasy. Conservative media built that fantasy over years. Jessica Foster just moved in. And while they were falling in love with a jpeg in the mini skirt, someone was running in only fans. Use the name at Jessica next door. Bio public servant by day, troublemaker by night. By the way, I respond to every message, but be patient since I'm not a robot. Ha ha, not a robot. Foot pictures, a hundred dollars of post paid for by the same guys thinking her for her service in the comments. She was thinking them for their service to her cash flow. This is what happens when you replace critical thinking with vibes. You end up with 657,000 likes on the fake soldier and heels at a real presidential event with the wrong name on the podium. The receipts were there the whole time. Nobody looked. Receipts. Not speculation.
curatorial_text: A synthetic woman in heels and a mini-skirt stands at a presidential podium. The podium reads BORDER OF PEACE. Donald Trump sits behind her. The post clears 657,000 likes. The image is a full-synthesis composite inserting a fabricated Army soldier, Jessica Foster, into a real White House summit; the same persona runs an OnlyFans account under the handle jessicanextdoor, charging a hundred dollars for foot pictures with the tagline not a robot. The interesting thing about this artifact is not its forgery, which is bad. The uniform is a costume, the rank insignia are wrong, the first name sits where a surname belongs, and the conference title is misspelled on the podium she physically occupies. The interesting thing is that the errors do not function as errors. They sit in plain sight and the audience routes around them, because the picture is not being parsed for accuracy; it is being parsed for vibe alignment, patriotic, military, pro-Trump, female, available. The AI did not manufacture the demand. Conservative media spent a decade pre-rendering this fantasy. Jessica Foster simply moved into the finished apartment and monetized the rent. The cheapest deepfakes win when the audience has already done the hard work of believing.

title: Pete Hegseth troll deepfake (yayneabeba)
curatorial_text: The Secretary of Defense's mouth moves, and what comes out is exactly the kind of thing his critics want to hear him say. The clip is short, low-stakes by the standards of statecraft, and circulates as a meme during a bout of Iran/US escalation. A lip-sync forgery grafted onto authentic Pentagon B-roll, the cabinet face left intact, the audio doing the lying. The artifact's significance is not that anyone mistakes it for a real briefing. It is that the genre of the political troll video has fully absorbed deepfake technique as a default tool, on par with a caption or a meme template. Where 2022 satirists labored to make a Zelensky or a Pelosi puppet, a 2026 anonymous account does it on a phone between dunks. The meme economy now treats a sitting cabinet secretary's likeness as raw material, the way an earlier internet treated a stock photo. The line between editorial cartoon and synthetic impersonation has not blurred; it has been paved over.
